title: Transfer of Undertakings (Protection of Employment) Regulations 2006
chapters: SI 2006/246 · Made 6 February 2006 · In Force 6 April 2006 · Replaces TUPE 1981 (SI 1981/1794) · Amended by Collective Redundancies and Transfer of Undertakings (Protection of Employment) (Amendment) Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/16) · Implements EU Acquired Rights Directive 2001/23/EC
desc: The Transfer of Undertakings (Protection of Employment) Regulations 2006 protect employees' terms and conditions when a business or undertaking (or part of one) is transferred to a new employer. TUPE operates by automatically transferring the contracts of employment of affected employees from the old employer (transferor) to the new employer (transferee). Employees transfer on their existing terms and conditions, and dismissal by reason of the transfer is automatically unfair unless there is an economic, technical or organisational reason entailing changes in the workforce. The Regulations impose information and consultation obligations on both the transferor and transferee. TUPE applies to both business transfers (regulation 3(1)(a)) and service provision changes (regulation 3(1)(b)). The 2014 amendments introduced micro-business consultation flexibilities and permitted changes to terms and conditions where the contract permits variation for reasons unconnected with the transfer. TUPE is critical for the facilities management sector — the Northerly Hill demo entity operates in FM where service provision changes are routine.

parts.title: Transfer of Undertakings — Application and Scope

sections.title: Interpretation
sections.summary: Defines key terms used throughout the Regulations. 'Relevant transfer' means a transfer or service provision change to which the Regulations apply. 'Transferor' means the person who ceases to carry on the business or undertaking. 'Transferee' means the person who assumes responsibility. 'Assigned' means assigned other than on a temporary basis. 'Employee' has the meaning given by section 230(1) of ERA 1996. The definition of 'organised grouping of employees' is central to service provision change claims.

versions.description: Original TUPE 2006 definitions. Replaced TUPE 1981 definitions with expanded scope including service provision changes.
2014 Amendment Regulations (SI 2014/16) amended definitions and introduced concept of micro-business (fewer than 10 employees) for consultation flexibility.

refs: reg.3 TUPE 2006 (application of Regulations — relevant transfer)

sections.title: A relevant transfer
sections.summary: Defines the two types of relevant transfer to which TUPE applies. First, a business transfer (reg.3(1)(a)): a transfer of an economic entity which retains its identity, consistent with the Acquired Rights Directive. Second, a service provision change (reg.3(1)(b)): where activities cease to be carried out by one person and are carried out instead by another (outsourcing, insourcing, or re-tendering). The service provision change concept is a UK innovation not required by the Directive. A relevant transfer does not include a transfer by share sale, a transfer of assets only, or a situation where activities are carried out in connection with a single specific event or task of short-term duration.
sections.text:
(1) These Regulations apply to—
(a) a transfer of an undertaking, business or part of an undertaking or business situated immediately before the transfer in the United Kingdom to another person where there is a transfer of an economic entity which retains its identity;
(b) a service provision change, that is a situation in which—
(i) activities cease to be carried out by a person ("a client") on his own behalf and are carried out instead by another person on the client's behalf ("a contractor"),
(ii) activities cease to be carried out by a contractor on a client's behalf (whether or not those activities had previously been carried out by the client on his own behalf) and are carried out instead by another person ("a subsequent contractor") on the client's behalf, or
(iii) activities cease to be carried out by a contractor or a subsequent contractor on a client's behalf (whether or not those activities had previously been carried out by the client on his own behalf) and are carried out instead by the client on his own behalf,
and in which the conditions set out in paragraph (3) are satisfied.

(2) In this regulation "economic entity" means an organised grouping of resources which has the objective of pursuing an economic activity, whether or not that activity is central or ancillary.

(3) The conditions referred to in paragraph (1)(b) are that—
(a) immediately before the service provision change—
(i) there is an organised grouping of employees situated in Great Britain which has as its principal purpose the carrying out of the activities concerned on behalf of the client;
(ii) the client intends that the activities will, following the service provision change, be carried out by the transferee other than in connection with a single specific event or task of short-term duration; and
(b) the activities concerned do not consist wholly or mainly of the supply of goods for the client's use.

(4) Subject to paragraph (1), these Regulations apply to—
(a) public and private undertakings engaged in economic activities whether or not they are operating for gain;
(b) a transfer or service provision change howsoever effected.

versions.description: Original provision. Introduced service provision change (SPC) concept alongside traditional business transfer. SPC was a significant UK extension beyond the minimum required by the Acquired Rights Directive.

2014 Amendments confirmed retention of service provision change provisions following government consultation. The Coalition Government had considered abolishing SPC but retained it after employer and union representations.

leadingCases.name: Spijkers v Gebroeders Benedik Abattoir CV [1986] ECJ Case 24/85
leadingCases.heldText: The ECJ established the multifactorial test for determining whether a transfer of an economic entity has occurred. The decisive criterion is whether the entity retains its identity, assessed by reference to factors including transfer of tangible and intangible assets, transfer of workforce, transfer of customers, similarity of activities, and period of interruption.
leadingCases.significance: Foundation authority for the multifactorial test applied in all business transfer cases

leadingCases.name: Cheesman v R Brewer Contracts Ltd [2001] IRLR 144
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT summarised the principles from ECJ case law into a comprehensive checklist for determining whether a relevant transfer has occurred. The tribunal must consider all the factual circumstances and no single factor is conclusive.
leadingCases.significance: Definitive UK checklist for assessing whether a TUPE transfer has occurred

leadingCases.name: Enterprise Management Services Ltd v Connect-Up Ltd [2012] IRLR 190
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that for a service provision change, there must be an organised grouping of employees whose principal purpose is carrying out the relevant activities. A single employee can constitute an organised grouping. The activities carried out after the SPC need not be identical but must be fundamentally or essentially the same.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority on the 'organised grouping' and 'fundamentally the same activities' tests for SPC

leadingCases.name: OCS Group UK Ltd v Jones [2009] All ER (D) 125
leadingCases.heldText: The question of whether there is a service provision change is one of fact for the employment tribunal. The tribunal must consider whether the activities before and after the change are fundamentally the same.
leadingCases.significance: Established the 'fundamentally the same' test for comparing activities in SPC cases

refs: Directive 2001/23/EC Art.1 (scope of the Acquired Rights Directive)
reg.4 TUPE 2006 (effect of transfer on contracts of employment)
reg.7 TUPE 2006 (dismissal because of relevant transfer)

parts.title: Effect of Relevant Transfer on Contracts of Employment

sections.title: Effect of relevant transfer on contracts of employment
sections.summary: The core operative provision of TUPE. On a relevant transfer, the contract of employment of any person employed by the transferor who is assigned to the organised grouping of resources or employees that is the subject of the transfer has effect after the transfer as if originally made between the employee and the transferee. All the transferor's rights, powers, duties and liabilities under or in connection with the contract transfer to the transferee. An employee may object to transferring, but this terminates the contract without it being treated as a dismissal. The employee loses continuity of employment if they object.
sections.text:
(1) Except where objection is made under paragraph (7), a relevant transfer shall not operate so as to terminate the contract of employment of any person employed by the transferor and assigned to the organised grouping of resources or employees that is subject to the relevant transfer, which would otherwise be terminated by the transfer, but any such contract shall have effect after the transfer as if originally made between the person so employed and the transferee.

(2) Without prejudice to paragraph (1), but subject to paragraph (6), and regulations 8 and 15(9), on the completion of a relevant transfer—
(a) all the transferor's rights, powers, duties and liabilities under or in connection with any such contract shall transfer by virtue of this regulation to the transferee; and
(b) any act or omission before the transfer is completed, of or in relation to the transferor in respect of that contract or a person assigned to that organised grouping of resources or employees, shall be deemed to have been an act or omission of or in relation to the transferee.

(3) Any reference in paragraph (1) to a person employed by the transferor and assigned to the organised grouping of resources or employees that is subject to a relevant transfer, is a reference to a person so employed immediately before the transfer, or who would have been so employed if he had not been dismissed in the circumstances described in regulation 7(1).

(4) Subject to regulation 9, any purported variation of a contract of employment that is, or will be, transferred by paragraph (1), is void if the sole or principal reason for the variation is the transfer.

(5) Paragraph (4) does not prevent a variation of the contract of employment if—
(a) the sole or principal reason for the variation is an economic, technical or organisational reason entailing changes in the workforce, provided that the employer and employee agree that variation; or
(b) the terms of that contract permit the employer to make such a variation.

(5A) In paragraph (5), the expression "changes in the workforce" includes a change to the place where employees are employed by the employer to carry on the business of the employer or to carry out work of a particular kind for the employer (and the reference to such a change is a reference to such a change after the transfer).

(7) Paragraphs (1) and (2) shall not operate to transfer the contract of employment and the rights, powers, duties and liabilities under or in connection with it of an employee who informs the transferor or the transferee that he objects to becoming employed by the transferee.

(8) Subject to paragraphs (9) and (11), where an employee so objects, the relevant transfer shall operate so as to terminate his contract of employment with the transferor but he shall not be treated, for any purpose, as having been dismissed by the transferor.

(9) Subject to regulation 9, where a relevant transfer involves or would involve a substantial change in working conditions to the material detriment of a person whose contract of employment is or would be transferred under paragraph (1), such an employee may treat the contract of employment as having been terminated, and the employee shall be treated for any purpose as having been dismissed by the employer.

(11) Paragraph (9) does not apply if the changes referred to in that paragraph are changes in the identity of the employer (unless the employee shows that, in all the circumstances, the change is a significant change and is to his detriment).

versions.description: Original provision replacing TUPE 1981 reg.5. Substantially expanded with objection right (para.7–8), material detriment constructive dismissal (para.9), and variation restrictions (para.4–5).

2014 Amendments inserted paragraph (5)(b) — permitting contractual variations where the contract itself permits the variation for reasons unconnected with the transfer. Also inserted paragraph (5A) expanding the definition of 'changes in the workforce' to include changes to the place of work.

leadingCases.name: Litster v Forth Dry Dock & Engineering Co Ltd [1990] 1 AC 546
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that where employees are dismissed before a transfer for a reason connected with the transfer, they should be treated as if they were still employed immediately before the transfer. The Regulations must be construed purposively to give effect to the underlying Directive.
leadingCases.significance: Established the purposive interpretation of TUPE and protection against pre-transfer dismissals

leadingCases.name: Daddy's Dance Hall (Foreningen af Arbejdsledere i Danmark v Daddy's Dance Hall A/S) [1988] ECJ Case 324/86
leadingCases.heldText: Employees cannot waive the rights conferred on them by the Directive, and those rights cannot be restricted even with the employee's consent. The terms and conditions of employment transfer automatically and the parties cannot agree to reduce them by reason of the transfer.
leadingCases.significance: Established that TUPE rights cannot be waived or reduced by agreement

leadingCases.name: Humphreys v University of Oxford [2000] ICR 405
leadingCases.heldText: An employee who objects to the transfer under regulation 4(7) is not treated as dismissed. The contract terminates by operation of law but the employee has no claim for unfair dismissal or redundancy payment. However, if the transfer involves a substantial change in working conditions to the employee's material detriment, the employee can claim constructive dismissal under regulation 4(9).
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the consequences of employee objection to transfer

refs: reg.9 TUPE 2006 (variations of contract where transferors are in insolvency proceedings)

sections.title: Dismissal of employee because of relevant transfer
sections.summary: Where a relevant transfer is the sole or principal reason for dismissal, the dismissal is automatically unfair unless the employer can show that the reason was an economic, technical or organisational reason entailing changes in the workforce (an 'ETO reason'). Where an ETO reason is established, the dismissal is treated as being for redundancy or some other substantial reason — but the employer must still show that the dismissal was fair in all the circumstances under ERA 1996 s.98(4).
sections.text:
(1) Where either before or after a relevant transfer, any employee of the transferor or transferee is dismissed, that employee shall be treated for the purposes of Part X of the 1996 Act (unfair dismissal) as unfairly dismissed if the sole or principal reason for his dismissal is—
(a) the transfer itself; or
(b) a reason connected with the transfer that is not an economic, technical or organisational reason entailing changes in the workforce.

(2) This paragraph applies where the sole or principal reason for the dismissal is a reason connected with the transfer that is an economic, technical or organisational reason entailing changes in the workforce of either the transferor or the transferee before or after a relevant transfer.

(3) Where paragraph (2) applies—
(a) paragraph (1) shall not apply;
(b) without prejudice to the application of section 98(4) of the 1996 Act (test of fairness), the dismissal shall, for the purposes of sections 98(1) and 135 of the 1996 Act (reason for dismissal), be regarded as having been for—
(i) a reason falling within section 98(2)(c) of the 1996 Act (redundancy), or
(ii) a substantial reason of a kind such as to justify the dismissal of an employee holding the position which that employee held.

(3A) For the purposes of paragraph (2), the expression "changes in the workforce" includes a change to the place where employees are employed by the employer to carry on the business of the employer or to carry out work of a particular kind for the employer (and the reference to such a change is a reference to such a change after the transfer).

(4) The provisions of this regulation apply irrespective of whether the employee in question is assigned to the organised grouping of resources or employees that is, or will be, transferred.

versions.description: Original provision replacing TUPE 1981 reg.8. Expanded ETO reason definition.
2014 Amendments inserted paragraph (3A) expanding 'changes in the workforce' to include changes to the place of work, mirroring the amendment to reg.4(5A).

leadingCases.name: Berriman v Delabole Slate Ltd [1985] ICR 546
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that 'changes in the workforce' means changes in the numbers or functions of the workforce, not merely changes to their terms and conditions. A harmonisation of terms and conditions is not an ETO reason because it does not entail changes in the workforce.
leadingCases.significance: Established the narrow construction of 'changes in the workforce' as requiring changes in numbers or functions

leadingCases.name: Warner v Adnet Ltd [1998] ICR 1056
leadingCases.heldText: The ETO reason defence requires the employer to show that the reason entailed changes in the workforce. The changes must be an objective of the employer's plan, not merely a consequence of it.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified that workforce changes must be an objective, not a mere consequence, of the employer's plan

leadingCases.name: Manchester College v Hazel [2014] EWCA Civ 72
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that an employer who dismisses employees before a transfer for a reason connected with the transfer bears the burden of proving that the dismissal was for an ETO reason. The transferee can rely on an ETO reason even where the dismissals were effected by the transferor before the transfer.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed burden of proof on employer for ETO reason and that transferee can rely on transferor's ETO reason

refs: ERA 1996 s.98(1)–(4) (reason for dismissal and test of fairness)

sections.title: Notification of employee liability information
sections.summary: The transferor must notify the transferee of prescribed employee liability information not less than 28 days before the transfer (increased from 14 days by the 2014 amendments). This includes the identity and age of transferring employees, their terms and conditions, information about disciplinary and grievance actions in the previous two years, any legal actions brought against the transferor in the previous two years, and any collective agreements that will apply.

versions.description: Original provision. Required notification not less than 14 days before transfer.
2014 Amendments extended notification period from 14 to 28 days. Added requirement to include information about disciplinary and grievance procedures in the previous 2 years.

refs: reg.12 TUPE 2006 (remedy for failure to notify employee liability information — minimum £500 per employee)
ERA 1996 s.1 (written statement of employment particulars)

sections.title: Remedy for failure to notify employee liability information
sections.summary: If the transferor fails to comply with the obligation to notify employee liability information, the transferee may bring a complaint to an employment tribunal. The tribunal may award compensation of not less than £500 per employee in respect of whom the transferor has failed to comply, having regard to any loss sustained by the transferee and the terms of any agreement between the transferor and transferee relating to the failure.
sections.text:
(1) On or after a relevant transfer, the transferee may present a complaint to an employment tribunal that the transferor has failed to comply with any provision of regulation 11.

(2) An employment tribunal shall not consider a complaint under this regulation unless it is presented—
(a) before the end of the period of three months beginning with the date of the relevant transfer;
(b) within such further period as the tribunal considers reasonable in a case where it is satisfied that it was not reasonably practicable for the complaint to be presented before the end of that period of three months.

(3) Where an employment tribunal finds a complaint under paragraph (1) well-founded, the tribunal—
(a) shall make a declaration to that effect; and
(b) may make an award of such sum as the tribunal considers just and equitable in all the circumstances, having particular regard to—
(i) any loss sustained by the transferee which is attributable to the matters complained of; and
(ii) the terms of any agreement between the transferor and the transferee relating to the transfer under which the transferor may be liable to pay any sum to the transferee in respect of a failure to notify the transferee of employee liability information.

(4) The amount of the award under paragraph (3)(b) shall be not less than £500 per employee in respect of whom the transferor has failed to comply with a provision of regulation 11, unless the tribunal considers it just and equitable, in all the circumstances, to award a lesser sum.

versions.description: Original provision establishing the £500 minimum per employee for failure to provide ELI.

refs: reg.11 TUPE 2006 (duty to notify employee liability information)

sections.title: Duty to inform and consult representatives
sections.summary: Both the transferor and transferee must inform and consult appropriate representatives of affected employees. Information must include the fact that the transfer is to take place, when it is to take place and why, the legal, economic and social implications for affected employees, and any measures the employer envisages taking in connection with the transfer. Where measures are envisaged, consultation must take place with a view to seeking agreement. Representatives may be trade union representatives or elected employee representatives. The 2014 amendments permitted micro-businesses (fewer than 10 employees) to inform and consult directly with affected employees where there are no existing appropriate representatives.
sections.text:
(1) In this regulation and regulations 14 and 15 references to affected employees, in relation to a relevant transfer, are to any employees of the transferor or the transferee (whether or not assigned to the organised grouping of resources or employees that is the subject of a relevant transfer) who may be affected by the transfer or may be affected by measures taken in connection with it.

(2) Long enough before a relevant transfer to enable the employer of any affected employees to consult the appropriate representatives of any affected employees, the employer shall inform those representatives of—
(a) the fact that the transfer is to take place, the date or proposed date of the transfer and the reasons for it;
(b) the legal, economic and social implications of the transfer for any affected employees;
(c) the measures which he envisages he will, in connection with the transfer, take in relation to any affected employees or, if he envisages that no measures will be so taken, that fact; and
(d) if the employer is the transferor, the measures, in connection with the transfer, which he envisages the transferee will take in relation to any affected employees who will become employees of the transferee after the transfer by virtue of regulation 4 or, if he envisages that no measures will be so taken, that fact.

(6) An employer of an affected employee who envisages that he will take measures in relation to an affected employee, in connection with the relevant transfer, shall consult the appropriate representatives of that employee with a view to seeking their agreement to the intended measures.

(7) In the course of those consultations the employer shall—
(a) consider any representations made by the appropriate representatives; and
(b) reply to those representations and, if he rejects any of those representations, state his reasons.

(11) Where, in the case of a micro-business, there are no appropriate representatives within the meaning of paragraph (3), the employer may comply with this regulation by informing and consulting each of the affected employees directly.

versions.description: Original provision. Required information and consultation with appropriate representatives (trade union reps or elected employee reps).

2014 Amendments inserted paragraph (11) creating micro-business exception: employers with fewer than 10 employees may inform and consult directly with affected employees where no appropriate representatives exist.

leadingCases.name: Todd v Strain [2011] UKEAT/0057/11
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the duty to inform and consult arises 'long enough before' the transfer to enable meaningful consultation. What constitutes 'long enough' depends on the circumstances, including the complexity of the transfer and the measures envisaged.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the timing obligation for TUPE consultation

refs: reg.15 TUPE 2006 (failure to inform or consult — up to 13 weeks' pay per employee)
Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 s.188 (collective redundancy consultation — compare)

sections.title: Failure to inform or consult
sections.summary: Where an employer fails to comply with the duty to inform and consult under regulation 13, a complaint may be presented to an employment tribunal by the appropriate representatives, a trade union, or an affected employee. The tribunal may award compensation of up to 13 weeks' pay per affected employee. Both the transferor and transferee may be jointly and severally liable.
sections.text:
(1) Where an employer has failed to comply with a requirement of regulation 13 or regulation 14, a complaint may be presented to an employment tribunal on that ground—
(a) in the case of a failure relating to the election of employee representatives, by any of his employees who are affected employees;
(b) in the case of any other failure relating to employee representatives, by any of the employee representatives to whom the failure related;
(c) in the case of failure relating to representatives of a trade union, by the trade union; and
(d) in any other case, by any of his employees who are affected employees.

(7) Where the tribunal finds a complaint against a transferee under paragraph (1) well-founded it shall make a declaration to that effect and may order the transferee to pay appropriate compensation to such descriptions of affected employees as may be specified in the award.

(8) Where the tribunal finds a complaint against a transferor under paragraph (1) well-founded it shall make a declaration to that effect and may—
(a) order the transferor, subject to paragraph (9), to pay appropriate compensation to such descriptions of affected employees as may be specified in the award; or
(b) if the complaint is that the transferor did not perform the duty mentioned in paragraph (5) and the transferor (after giving due notice) shows that it was not reasonably practicable for him to perform that duty, make no order under sub-paragraph (a).

(9) The transferee shall be jointly and severally liable with the transferor in respect of compensation payable under paragraph (8)(a).

(10) An employee may present a complaint to an employment tribunal on the ground that he is an employee of a description to which an order under paragraph (7) or (8) relates and that the transferor or transferee has failed, wholly or in part, to pay him compensation in pursuance of such an order.

(11) "Appropriate compensation" in paragraph (7) or (8) means such sum not exceeding thirteen weeks' pay for the employee in question as the tribunal considers just and equitable having regard to the seriousness of the failure of the employer to comply with his duty.

versions.description: Original provision. Maximum compensation: 13 weeks' pay per affected employee.

leadingCases.name: Sweetin v Coral Racing [2006] IRLR 252
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the maximum award under regulation 15 is 13 weeks' actual pay per affected employee. The tribunal has discretion to award any sum up to this maximum. A complete failure to inform or consult is likely to result in an award at or near the maximum.
leadingCases.significance: Established that complete failure to consult warrants an award at or near the 13-week maximum

refs: reg.13 TUPE 2006 (duty to inform and consult)
TULRCA 1992 s.189 (protective award for failure to collectively consult on redundancies — compare)

sections.title: Pensions
sections.summary: Regulations 4 and 7 do not apply to occupational pension scheme rights. The transfer of employment under TUPE does not automatically transfer occupational pension scheme rights, obligations or liabilities. However, the Transfer of Employment (Pension Protection) Regulations 2005 require the transferee to provide minimum pension provision for transferring employees who had occupational pension scheme membership.
sections.text:
(1) Regulations 4 and 7 shall not apply—
(a) to so much of a contract of employment or collective agreement as relates to an occupational pension scheme (including any provision of an occupational pension scheme which does not relate to benefits for old age, invalidity or survivors); or
(b) to any rights, powers, duties or liabilities under or in connection with any such contract or subsisting by virtue of any such agreement and relating to such a scheme or otherwise arising in connection with that person's employment and relating to such a scheme.

(2) For the purposes of paragraphs (1) and (3)—
(a) any provisions of an occupational pension scheme which do not relate to benefits for old age, invalidity or survivors shall be treated as not being part of the scheme; and
(b) any provisions of an occupational pension scheme which relate to benefits for old age, invalidity or survivors shall not include any provision of such a scheme which relates to benefits for old age, invalidity or survivors which are supplementary to, or dependent on, other benefits payable under the scheme.

versions.description: Original provision. Confirmed pension exclusion from automatic transfer. Supersedes TUPE 1981 position.

leadingCases.name: Beckmann v Dynamco Whicheloe Macfarlane Ltd [2002] ECJ Case C-164/00
leadingCases.heldText: Benefits paid on redundancy or early retirement which are not 'old age, invalidity or survivors' benefits are not within the pensions exclusion. Such benefits (e.g. enhanced redundancy benefits calculated by reference to pension scheme provisions) do transfer under TUPE.
leadingCases.significance: Narrowed the pensions exclusion — only 'pure' old age, invalidity and survivors benefits are excluded from TUPE transfer

refs: Pensions Act 2004 s.258 (pension protection on TUPE transfer)

sections.title: Variations of contract where transferors are subject to relevant insolvency proceedings
sections.summary: Where the transferor is subject to relevant insolvency proceedings, the transferor and transferee may agree permitted variations to the contracts of employment of transferring employees. This relaxes the general prohibition on transfer-connected variations in regulation 4(4) for insolvency situations, provided the variations are designed to safeguard employment opportunities by ensuring the survival of the undertaking.
sections.text:
(1) If at the time of a relevant transfer the transferor is subject to relevant insolvency proceedings paragraphs (2) and (3) apply.

(2) The transferor or transferee (or an insolvency practitioner) and appropriate representatives of assigned employees may agree to permitted variations.

(7) For the purposes of this regulation a permitted variation is a variation to the contract of employment of an assigned employee where—
(a) the sole or principal reason for the variation is the transfer itself or a reason connected with the transfer that is not an economic, technical or organisational reason entailing changes in the workforce; and
(b) it is designed to safeguard employment opportunities by ensuring the survival of the undertaking, business or part of the undertaking or business that is the subject of the relevant transfer.

versions.description: Original provision. Created insolvency exception to the general prohibition on transfer-connected variations.

refs: reg.4(4)–(5) TUPE 2006 (general prohibition on transfer-connected variations)
reg.8 TUPE 2006 (insolvency — relevant statutory scheme debts)

pendingAmendments: Post-Brexit retained EU law — TUPE now assimilated law. Government retains power to amend under REUL Act 2023
No ERA 2025 amendments directly to TUPE, but fire-and-rehire restrictions (ERA 2025 s.131) interact with TUPE reg.4 variation provisions

parliamentaryOrigin.enablingAct: European Communities Act 1972 s.2(2) and Employment Relations Act 1999 s.38
parliamentaryOrigin.madeBy: Secretary of State for Trade and Industry
parliamentaryOrigin.note: TUPE 2006 replaced TUPE 1981 (SI 1981/1794). The 2006 Regulations were preceded by extensive DTI consultation (2001 and 2005). The most significant UK innovation was the service provision change concept (reg.3(1)(b)) — not required by the Directive but introduced to provide certainty for outsourcing, insourcing, and re-tendering situations. The 2014 Amendments (SI 2014/16) were made following further BIS consultation and introduced micro-business flexibilities and contractual variation provisions.

precedingDocuments.title: Acquired Rights Directive 2001/23/EC
precedingDocuments.significance: The governing EU Directive. Requires member states to protect employees' terms and conditions on transfer of an undertaking. TUPE 2006 implements the Directive in the UK.

precedingDocuments.title: TUPE 1981 (SI 1981/1794)
precedingDocuments.significance: The predecessor regulations replaced by TUPE 2006. TUPE 1981 implemented the original Acquired Rights Directive 77/187/EEC but did not include service provision changes.

precedingDocuments.title: DTI Consultation: TUPE Regulations 2006 — Public Consultation
precedingDocuments.significance: The 2005 consultation that preceded the TUPE 2006 Regulations. Key issues: service provision changes, information and consultation obligations, the ETO reason defence, and pension exclusions.

precedingDocuments.title: BIS Consultation: TUPE Amendment Regulations 2014
precedingDocuments.publisher: Department for Business, Innovation and Skills
precedingDocuments.significance: Consultation preceding the 2014 amendments. The Coalition Government considered abolishing service provision changes but retained them after representations. Introduced micro-business consultation flexibilities and contractual variation provisions.

leadingCases.name: Spijkers v Benedik Abattoir [1986] ECJ Case 24/85
leadingCases.heldText: Established the multifactorial test for whether a transfer has occurred — the entity must retain its identity.
leadingCases.significance: Foundation authority for the business transfer test.

leadingCases.name: Litster v Forth Dry Dock [1990] 1 AC 546
leadingCases.heldText: Employees dismissed before transfer for a transfer-connected reason should be treated as employed immediately before the transfer.
leadingCases.significance: Purposive interpretation — protection against pre-transfer dismissals.

leadingCases.name: Cheesman v R Brewer Contracts [2001] IRLR 144
leadingCases.heldText: Definitive UK checklist for assessing whether a TUPE transfer has occurred.
leadingCases.significance: The practical checklist applied in all business transfer cases.

leadingCases.name: Enterprise Management Services v Connect-Up [2012] IRLR 190
leadingCases.heldText: For SPC, there must be an organised grouping whose principal purpose is the relevant activities. A single employee suffices. Activities must be fundamentally the same.
leadingCases.significance: Key SPC authority — organised grouping and fundamentally same activities tests.

leadingCases.name: Daddy's Dance Hall [1988] ECJ Case 324/86
leadingCases.heldText: TUPE rights cannot be waived or reduced by agreement between the parties.
leadingCases.significance: Prohibition on consensual reduction of transferred terms.

leadingCases.name: Beckmann v Dynamco Whicheloe Macfarlane [2002] ECJ C-164/00
leadingCases.heldText: Early retirement and enhanced redundancy benefits linked to pension schemes DO transfer — only 'pure' old age, invalidity and survivors benefits are excluded.
leadingCases.significance: Narrowed the pensions exclusion.

leadingCases.name: Sweetin v Coral Racing [2006] IRLR 252
leadingCases.heldText: Complete failure to inform and consult warrants an award at or near the 13-week maximum.
leadingCases.significance: Maximum compensation starting point for consultation failure.
